assertion.title: Point mutation, constant perturbation, and memetic local search operators do not improve GP exact matches
assertion.statement: Adding point mutation (single-node operator swap), constant perturbation (small numeric adjustments with nice-constant snapping), and memetic local search (hill-climbing on top-10 individuals every 5 generations) with adaptive operator scheduling (explore-to-exploit shift) produces 24-25/50 exact matches across 4 full benchmark runs, indistinguishable from the 25/50 baseline. The new operators do not reliably improve or degrade performance. Additionally, adding inv (1/x) and cube unary operators to help with relativistic equations (Lorentz factor) hurt performance (22/50) due to search space bloat. The GP's inherent stochasticity (±1-2 exact matches per run) makes it difficult to measure marginal improvements from operator-level changes without multi-run averaging.

changes_made: Added constant_perturb(): small numeric adjustments, nice-constant snapping (pi, e, integers)
Added local_search(): hill-climbing on top-10 individuals every 5 generations
Added adaptive operator scheduling: crossover 0.70→0.60, point mutation 0.10→0.18 over generations
Reduced CROSSOVER_PROB from 0.70 to 0.60, split MUTATION_PROB into subtree (0.12) + point (0.10) + const_perturb (0.10)
Tested adding inv (1/x) and cube (x³) operators — hurt performance, reverted
key_observations: GP stochasticity (±1-2 exact matches/run) dominates signal from operator changes
inv operator was used by GP (eq23, eq31 expressions) but didn't reach correct Lorentz factor structure
Adding operators increases search space, offsetting any benefit from finer-grained mutation
Tier 3 relativistic equations remain hard — need 5-level nesting for 1/sqrt(1-v²/c²)
Borderline equations (eq05, eq21, eq27, eq42) flip between exact/non-exact across runs
